_patch_meta.session_ref: M10c Phase 2 Pass A meaning record via Claude API
_patch_meta.source: Claude API meaning record (Sonnet 4.6); 263 verses across 8 terms

set.analysis_note: A violent sexual act is named as defilement against a person; the inner impact is felt as violation of a daughter, prompting a father's restrained, simmering awareness of wrong done.
The defilement of a sister becomes the moral justification the brothers invoke inwardly to authorize deceit; the sense of violated honour drives a calculated, vengeful will.
The remembered defilement of a sister is cited as the moral cause that releases extreme retributive violence; the inner engine is a will acting from perceived obligation to avenge violated honour.
Contact with a ritually unclean object transmits an impurity the person may not immediately know; guilt surfaces when awareness arrives, indicating conscience awakening after hidden violation.
set.keywords: ["impurity contact", "violation inadvertent", "guilt surfacing", "conscience awakening", "awareness delayed", "ignorance real"]

set.analysis_note: Unknowing contact with human uncleanness defiles a person; the guilt becomes real only when the person becomes aware, showing that defilement can precede conscious recognition.
set.keywords: ["impurity contact", "violation inadvertent", "guilt surfacing", "conscience awakening", "awareness delayed", "ignorance real"]

set.analysis_note: Flesh touching an unclean thing becomes unfit for eating; the unclean state is transferred by contact and excludes from sacred participation, marking a boundary the clean person must guard.
Touching any unclean thing and then eating sacred offerings combines two violations; the inner failure to maintain purity results in the severest communal consequence — being cut off.
Priests are called to maintain an active inner discernment that distinguishes the unclean from the clean; the faculty required is moral-spiritual perception, not mere ritual habit.
Certain animals are declared inherently unclean; even touching their carcasses transmits impurity, establishing a boundary the person must hold by restraining contact.
Contact with forbidden carcasses renders a person unclean until evening; the defilement is temporal and bodily, affecting ritual standing through a specific act of touching.
Carrying a forbidden carcass extends defilement to clothing; the impurity spreads through the body's actions and must be addressed by washing, signalling external purification.
Touching hoofed animals that lack full cloven feet or do not chew cud renders one unclean; the impurity is incurred by contact with what is categorically designated as defiling.
These animals are declared unclean by category; their inherent impurity status makes them a standing threat to anyone who touches them, defining a class boundary for purity.
Animals walking on paws transmit defilement by contact with their carcasses; the person incurs a temporary ritual uncleanness requiring time and washing to resolve.
Paw-walking animals are categorically unclean; their status imposes a purity boundary that the person must respect to avoid incurring the same ritual state.
Carrying the carcass of unclean animals defiles the person's clothes as well as the person; the defilement is resolved only by washing and waiting, requiring active purification response.
These carcasses are declared unclean to the people; the declaration sets their inherent impurity status as the reason the person carrying them becomes defiled.
Touching dead swarming creatures renders a person unclean until evening; the defilement is tied to death-contact and is temporary, requiring watchfulness over bodily actions.
Swarming creatures are declared unclean; their category status creates a field of impurity risk that anyone must avoid to preserve ritual standing.
Objects onto which a dead swarming creature falls become unclean; the defilement transfers across material surfaces, demanding purification before those objects can be used.
An earthenware vessel whose interior is contaminated by a swarming creature becomes entirely unclean and must be broken; the defilement is irreversible in that material, demanding destruction.
Food and drink that contact water from a defiled vessel become unclean; the impurity transmits through liquid, extending the contamination field to sustenance.
Objects on which carcass parts fall are rendered persistently unclean and must be destroyed; the uncleanness is emphatic and irreversible for these items, requiring radical removal.
The ovens and stoves are declared unclean and must remain so until destroyed; their permanent unclean status demands removal rather than restoration, underscoring severity of defilement.

A spring or cistern of water remains clean despite contact with carcasses, but a person who touches that carcass within it becomes unclean; the distinction shows defilement is person-specific, not universal.
Wet seed that a carcass touches becomes unclean; moisture serves as a conductor of impurity, placing responsibility on the person to guard what they allow contact with their provisions.
Even a permitted animal, when dead, defiles by touch; the death-state creates impurity regardless of the animal's clean category, requiring the person to remain alert to death-contact.
Eating or carrying a permitted animal's carcass defiles the person and their clothing; the defilement requires washing and waiting, linking bodily action to purity obligation.
Willful defilement through swarming things is addressed as a matter of volitional self-corruption; the person is urged to refuse it, making this a choice of the will concerning personal holiness.
Divine holiness is the explicit ground for refusing self-defilement; the person's will to abstain from defiling contact is tied to imitating the holy character of God.
The purpose of all purity laws is framed as cultivating active moral discernment — distinguishing clean from unclean is a perceptual and volitional discipline central to covenant life.
Childbirth renders a woman unclean for seven days, analogous to menstrual impurity; the defilement is physiological in origin, not moral fault, producing a temporary ritual status.
The post-birth state is likened to the niddah condition of menstruation; the bodily discharge defines a periodic impurity cycle tied to the body's reproductive processes.
A female birth extends the unclean period to two weeks; the duration is doubled, calibrated to bodily process, not moral failure, sustaining the physiological impurity framework.
The double-length post-birth impurity is framed in terms of the niddah standard for menstruation; the bodily discharge paradigm governs the categorization of this longer impure period.
A priestly examination of skin disease results in a pronouncement of uncleanness; the defilement is assessed externally by authority, with the person's bodily state determining ritual standing.
A spreading skin eruption prompts a priestly verdict of uncleanness; the progressive worsening of a bodily condition escalates the ritual impurity status.
Chronic skin disease results in an immediate unclean verdict without confinement; the persistent, established nature of the disease makes the person's unclean state open and undeniable.
The person with chronic skin disease is declared unclean as a settled fact; the unclean status is both a moral-ritual verdict and a social reality that removes him from community.
The appearance of raw flesh immediately triggers an unclean verdict; visible bodily deterioration is the direct indicator of an impurity that requires no further discernment.
Raw flesh is inherently unclean and the priest's examination confirms this; the state of the body's surface directly determines ritual standing with no ambiguity.
Raw flesh is declared unclean as a category; the inherent impurity of exposed diseased flesh defines the ground for the priest's ruling and the person's exclusion.
A skin condition in a boil site that meets diagnostic criteria receives an unclean verdict; bodily location and observable symptoms together determine the impurity pronouncement.
Spreading of a suspicious skin mark leads to a priestly verdict of uncleanness; progressive worsening of a bodily condition confirms and necessitates the impurity declaration.
A burn site showing signs of leprosy results in an unclean verdict; the bodily wound site becomes a locus of impurity assessment, confirming the disease and the person's ritual standing.
A spreading suspicious spot examined on the seventh day receives an unclean verdict; the temporal re-examination confirms that progressive spread is the decisive indicator of impurity.
A scalp or beard itch with yellow thin hair is declared unclean by the priest; the precise bodily markers on the head or face determine an impurity verdict affecting community standing.
A spreading itch alone is sufficient to declare a person unclean without waiting for yellow hair; the advanced spread of the condition confirms the unclean status as self-evident.
A man with leprous disease on his head is declared unclean; the priest's pronouncement is both a confirmation and a public verdict that establishes the person's excluded identity.
The leprous man's unclean state is declared emphatically, with the disease located on his head; the verdict is total and public, shaping his identity as one excluded from clean community.

The person bearing disease must publicly declare their own unclean state — the inner reality of contamination is verbalized outward through ritual cry, marking a condition that isolates the self from the community.

The disease renders the person persistently unclean for its entire duration, requiring physical separation from community — uncleanness here is a sustained state that defines social and relational exclusion.
While the disease persists the person is declared unclean and must dwell apart — the unclean state is ongoing, tied to visible bodily condition, and produces enforced relational isolation.

A spreading disease in fabric after seven days is declared a persistent leprous defilement — the unclean verdict here attaches to the object's progressive corruption rather than to a person's inner state.

An unwashed-looking diseased patch that has not spread still remains unclean and must be burned — the unclean verdict persists even without visible growth, indicating that unchanged corruption is still corruption.
This verse summarizes the law for diagnosing leprous disease in fabric — the clean/unclean determination is a juridical outcome of priestly examination, with no direct inner-being content evidenced.
set.keywords: ["status determined", "examination priestly", "verdict clean unclean", "law summarized", "diagnosis ritual"]

set.analysis_note: Objects in a diseased house are emptied before priestly inspection to prevent them being declared unclean — the concern is contamination spreading by association before an official verdict, with no direct inner-being content.
Diseased stones from a house are removed and thrown to an unclean place outside the city — defilement transfers from object to location, quarantining contagion away from inhabited space.
Scraped plaster from a diseased house is deposited in an unclean place outside the city — the unclean designation marks a receptacle zone for removed contamination, extending impurity spatially.
set.keywords: ["contamination deposited", "spatial impurity", "removal ritual", "place designated unclean", "defilement contained"]

set.analysis_note: A house where disease has spread after repairs is declared persistently unclean — the verdict names the structure itself as the bearer of irreversible defilement requiring demolition.

The condemned house and all its materials are carried to an unclean place outside the city — the unclean designation governs the disposal site for what cannot be purified, spatially containing defilement.
Anyone who enters a shut-up diseased house becomes unclean until evening — the defilement transfers to the person by mere entry, showing contamination spreads through proximity and contact.
This verse closes the law on leprous disease by naming its purpose: to instruct when something is unclean versus clean — the unclean category is a legal/ritual determination governing discernment.
set.keywords: ["discernment clean unclean", "law purposive", "verdict ritual", "determination priestly", "status taught"]

set.analysis_note: A bodily discharge from a man renders him unclean — the source of uncleanness is a physical emission from the body itself, making the body the locus of the defiled state.
Any surface the person with a discharge lies or sits on becomes unclean — defilement transmits from body to object through sustained physical contact, making contamination radiative.
Touching the bed of one with a discharge transmits uncleanness requiring washing — secondary contact defiles, and the unclean state persists until evening after ritual cleansing.
Sitting on any surface used by one with a discharge defiles — contamination spreads through indirect bodily contact with objects, requiring washing and carrying a time-limited unclean state.
Direct physical touch of the body of one with a discharge transfers uncleanness — the body itself is the agent of defilement, and contact makes the other person ritually unclean until evening.
Even spitting on a clean person by one with a discharge defiles — the contaminating medium here is saliva, extending defilement beyond touch to any bodily fluid contact.
set.keywords: ["fluid contact", "defilement extended", "saliva impurity", "body fluid transmitting", "cleansing required"]

set.analysis_note: Any saddle ridden by the person with a discharge becomes unclean — defilement extends to objects used by the body in motion, showing contamination is not limited to direct skin contact.
set.keywords: ["object contaminated", "use defiles", "body impurity radiating", "saddle unclean", "contact indirect"]

set.analysis_note: Touching anything beneath the person with a discharge, or carrying such items, transmits uncleanness — defilement spreads through both contact and transport, making the carrier an agent of impurity.
If the person with a discharge touches someone without first washing hands, that person becomes unclean — the unwashed hand is the vector; ritual washing could have prevented the transfer.
A man's seminal emission renders him unclean until evening after bathing — the body's own natural emission creates a temporary defiled state requiring ritual cleansing before restoration.
set.keywords: ["emission bodily", "defilement temporary", "cleansing required", "body self contaminating", "restoration ritual"]

set.analysis_note: Garments or skin touched by semen are unclean until evening after washing — the defiling quality of the emission extends to objects it contacts, requiring their ritual washing.
Sexual intercourse resulting in emission makes both partners unclean until evening — intimate bodily union shares the defiled state, requiring mutual ritual washing before restoration.
A menstruating woman is unclean for seven days, and whoever touches her receives the same unclean status — the defilement is body-sourced and transferable through contact, lasting until evening.
set.keywords: ["body sourced", "menstrual defilement", "touch transmitting", "duration seven days", "contact impurity"]

set.analysis_note: The woman's menstrual blood discharge places her in a state of impurity — the niddah condition is defined by bodily blood flow, marking a ritual separateness tied to the body's cyclic state.
Everything a menstruating woman lies or sits on during her impurity becomes unclean — the niddah state radiates outward to all surfaces she occupies, extending contamination through use.

The niddah (menstrual impurity) period governs what becomes unclean through her lying or sitting — the impurity is a time-bound bodily state whose contaminating reach covers all surfaces she contacts.
Touching her bed during menstrual impurity transmits uncleanness — secondary contact with an object she has used defiles, requiring washing and carrying a temporary unclean state.
Touching anything she has sat on while unclean transmits defilement — the impurity travels from person to object to person, requiring ritual washing and carrying a time-limited unclean status.
Whether bed or seat, touching any surface she used during impurity defiles until evening — the contamination is consistent across all objects associated with her during the niddah period.
A man who has intercourse with a menstruating woman takes on her unclean state for seven full days — intimate union transfers the full weight of her niddah condition to him.
set.keywords: ["niddah transferred", "intimate union", "defilement received", "duration seven days", "full contamination"]

set.analysis_note: The menstrual impurity (niddah) passes to the man who lies with her, making him unclean seven days — the niddah condition is transmissible through sexual contact, not merely proximity.
set.keywords: ["niddah transmissible", "sexual contact", "impurity received", "duration seven days", "condition transferred"]

set.analysis_note: A woman with an abnormal prolonged blood discharge beyond her normal period is unclean for the entire duration — her unclean state is continuous and governed by the ongoing bodily condition, like her normal niddah.
set.keywords: ["discharge prolonged", "uncleanness continuous", "duration governed", "bodily condition ongoing", "niddah extended"]

set.analysis_note: The niddah (impurity) category applies to abnormal discharge that extends beyond the regular menstrual time — the concept stretches to cover any period of blood-sourced bodily impurity, not just monthly cycles.

Every surface the woman uses during her extended discharge is unclean as during regular menstrual impurity — the contaminating reach of the prolonged discharge mirrors that of the standard niddah state.

The bed she uses during her extended discharge is treated as her impurity (niddah) bed — the niddah framework governs all bodily-discharge impurity, unifying the category under a single ritual principle.
Anyone who touches objects used by a woman with extended discharge becomes unclean — the contamination continues to radiate outward to those who contact her objects, requiring washing.

Keeping people separate from their uncleanness prevents death — here defilement is existentially dangerous because it threatens to contaminate the divine dwelling; uncleanliness risks destroying the sacred relationship.
set.keywords: ["defilement fatal", "sacred threatened", "separation protective", "tabernacle endangered", "uncleanness relational cost"]

set.analysis_note: This verse summarizes the law for those rendered unclean by discharge or semen emission — the defiled state is a legal category arising from bodily emissions, closing the code's section on bodily impurity.
The unclean category covers the menstruating woman, anyone with a discharge, and the man who lies with an unclean woman — the term gathers all bodily-fluid-related impurity under one legal heading.

Menstrual impurity (niddah) here designates the condition of the unwell woman and governs the broader law — the niddah state is the paradigm case that organizes the entire impurity code for bodily discharge.

Eating an animal that died naturally or was killed by a predator makes one unclean — defilement enters through ingestion of prohibited food, requiring washing and carrying a time-limited unclean state.
Sexual approach to a menstruating woman is forbidden — the niddah state marks her as ritually set apart, and violation of that boundary constitutes a moral transgression, not merely ritual oversight.
set.keywords: ["niddah boundary", "approach forbidden", "moral transgression", "sexual violation", "boundary set apart"]

set.analysis_note: Lying with a neighbor's wife makes oneself unclean — adultery here is named as a self-defiling act; the defilement is morally self-inflicted through a wilful sexual violation of another's covenant bond.
Sexual union with an animal is named as self-defilement and perversion — the act corrupts the person inwardly by violating the created order; the defilement is both moral and ontological.
set.keywords: ["perversion defiles", "self corruption", "created order violated", "moral defilement", "will transgressing"]

set.analysis_note: The Israelites are commanded not to defile themselves by any of the listed sexual practices — this verse frames all the preceding prohibitions as self-defilement, warning that entire peoples have been corrupted by them.

Taking a brother's wife is named as impurity (niddah) — the term here shifts from bodily discharge to moral-relational violation; the marital transgression is categorized as a defilement of the family bond.
set.keywords: ["niddah moral", "relational violation", "family bond defiled", "transgression categorized", "impurity ethical"]

set.analysis_note: Israel is commanded to maintain the distinction between clean and unclean animals — the unclean category here functions as a moral-identity boundary; blurring it by eating unclean creatures renders the person detestable.
set.keywords: ["distinction maintained", "identity boundary", "unclean animals", "defilement by eating", "will choosing clean"]

set.analysis_note: A state of ritual uncleanness (from disease, discharge, corpse contact, or semen emission) bars a priest's son from eating holy things until cleansing occurs, treating uncleanness as a condition incompatible with sacred access.

Uncleanness contracted through corpse contact, discharge, or skin disease disqualifies a person from remaining within the community camp, marking uncleanness as a social and sacred boundary condition.
Defiling persons are removed from the camp precisely because God dwells there, directly linking defilement to incompatibility with divine presence in the inner community space.
A woman who has secretly committed adultery has defiled herself even without witnesses; the defilement is a moral-relational reality independent of human detection, residing in the act itself.
Jealousy aroused in a husband may correspond to actual defilement by his wife or may not; the verse distinguishes the subjective passion of jealousy from the objective fact of defilement.
Going astray from under a husband's authority and lying with another man constitutes self-defilement through betrayal of a covenantal bond, framing defilement as volitional moral defection.
If the woman truly defiled herself through broken faith, the ordeal water produces physical judgment, showing defilement as a moral-covenantal breach that carries objective consequences in the body.
If the woman has not defiled herself, she is acquitted and blessed with children, positioning the absence of defilement as moral integrity that results in life and freedom.
This law governs the case where a wife under authority goes astray and defiles herself, encoding defilement as the inner moral consequence of sexual transgression within a covenantal relationship.
A Nazirite must not defile himself even for the death of a close family member because his consecration to God overrides natural kinship bonds, placing the will's commitment above emotional impulse.

Sudden accidental proximity to a corpse defiles the Nazirite's consecrated head even without intention, showing that defilement can invade consecration involuntarily, requiring renewed cleansing and rededication.

Because the Nazirite's prior period of separation was defiled, it is counted void and must be restarted with a guilt offering, showing that defilement nullifies consecrated inner dedication retroactively.

Men rendered unclean by corpse contact cannot keep the Passover on the appointed day, showing uncleanness as a state that bars participation in communal worship, not a moral failing but a disqualifying condition.
The men self-identify as unclean through corpse contact and express grief at being excluded from the Lord's offering, showing a desire for worship thwarted by an involuntary impure state.

Provision is made for those unclean through corpse contact or distant travel to still keep Passover at a secondary time, showing accommodation for uncleanness as an involuntary barrier rather than a punishable offense.
Even the priest who performs the purification rite becomes temporarily unclean until evening, showing defilement as a transmissible state that attaches even to those performing holy service.
The one who burns the purification heifer also becomes unclean until evening through the burning itself, underscoring that even purification-related actions transmit a temporary unclean state.

The ashes of the sin-offering heifer are preserved as a resource specifically designated for cleansing impurity, establishing that impurity is a persistent community-wide problem requiring a standing remedy.

The ash-gatherer also becomes unclean until evening, and this statute applies perpetually to Israelites and sojourners alike, establishing uncleanness as a universal condition requiring ongoing attention.
Any contact with a human corpse renders a person unclean for seven days, establishing death-contact as the primary source of a substantial, time-bound impure state.

Failure to cleanse after corpse contact not only leaves uncleanness on the person but extends it to defile the tabernacle, making willful non-cleansing an act of communal and sacred corruption deserving excision.

The person who neglects purification remains in a persistent unclean state—'his uncleanness is still on him'—showing uncleanness as a clinging, unresolved condition when the appointed remedy is bypassed.
The water for impurity was the appointed remedy; not having it applied leaves the person's impurity intact, treating impurity as an objective condition that only the designated cleansing can remove.
Anyone sharing a tent with a corpse becomes unclean for seven days, showing that spatial proximity to death transmits defilement even without direct physical contact.
Any open, uncovered vessel in a tent with a corpse is unclean, extending the principle of impurity to inanimate objects that are vulnerable through lack of covering.
Open-field contact with a violent death, natural death, human bones, or a grave all produce the same seven-day uncleanness, showing that any encounter with death's remains defiles equally.
The purification water is specifically designated for those in an unclean state, confirming uncleanness as the condition that the cleansing rite addresses.
The unclean person receives a double sprinkling on specific days and then washes, showing the process by which an unclean state is progressively resolved through prescribed ritual acts.

Willful refusal to cleanse oneself after defilement results in being cut off from the assembly because the unclean person has actively defiled the sanctuary, making the will's refusal the decisive moral fault.
The man who is unclean and chooses not to cleanse himself remains unclean and is cut off; the persistence of the unclean state here flows from a deliberate will to remain in it.

The water for impurity that was not applied is the reason the person's defilement remains and the sanctuary suffers; impurity here is an objective pollution that the ordained remedy alone can neutralize.
Even those who sprinkle or touch the impurity water become unclean until evening, so the very medium used to remove defilement imparts a lesser temporary defilement to its handlers.

The impurity water is both the cure for deep uncleanness and a source of minor temporary uncleanness for those who handle it, revealing impurity's power to transmit itself even through cleansing agents.
Whatever an unclean person touches becomes unclean, and whoever touches that object also becomes unclean, showing defilement as a cascading, transferable state that spreads through contact chains.
The unclean person's touch renders objects and subsequent persons unclean until evening, making uncleanness a contagious state that propagates outward from one source through secondary contact.
Captured goods must be purified by water for impurity in addition to fire, establishing that impurity requires its own specific purging agent beyond mere physical cleansing.
The land must not be defiled because God dwells among the people in it; defilement here is a moral-sacral pollution of shared space that violates divine habitation.
In the context of non-sanctuary slaughter for ordinary eating, both ritually unclean and clean persons may eat alike, showing that uncleanness here does not affect access to non-sacred food.
Game animals eaten as ordinary food may be consumed by unclean and clean persons alike, indicating that uncleanness only restricts sacred eating, not common eating.
Defective animals not suitable for offering may be eaten by unclean and clean alike in ordinary meals, again marking the boundary of uncleanness's restriction as sacred not secular eating.
Leaving an executed body overnight on a tree defiles the land God is giving as inheritance; the command to bury the same day shows that defilement of sacred communal space must be actively prevented.

A woman who remarries after divorce and whose second husband divorces or dies is considered defiled for her first husband, and returning to him would be abominable and bring sin on the land—linking defilement to moral-relational corruption that contaminates the sacred inheritance.
The worshiper declares he did not remove tithe while in an unclean state, presenting freedom from uncleanness at the time of sacred giving as a matter of personal integrity and obedience.
The eastern tribes' land is characterized as unclean in contrast to the land where the tabernacle stands, treating geographical separation from sacred space as a form of ritual-moral deficiency.
The mother of the consecrated child is commanded to eat nothing unclean, showing that the unclean state must be avoided by those bearing or nurturing a person set apart for God.
A reforming king deliberately defiles the high places to render them permanently unfit for illicit worship, using ritual defilement as an instrument of iconoclastic purification.
set.keywords: ["defilement instrumental", "idolatry dismantled", "will reforming", "holy places corrupted", "worship destroyed"]

set.analysis_note: The king deliberately defiles the site of child sacrifice to Molech, rendering it permanently unfit for that practice; defilement is wielded intentionally to break a pattern of extreme moral-religious corruption.

The king defiles the high places built for foreign gods near Jerusalem, demonstrating that reforming will can turn the instrument of defilement against places of accumulated idolatrous moral corruption.
Burning bones on the altar defiles it according to prophetic word, showing defilement used purposefully as a fulfilment of divine judgment to permanently desecrate a site of false worship.
Gatekeepers are stationed at the temple to bar anyone unclean from entering, showing vigilant institutional will protecting the sacred space from defilement as an ongoing duty.

The call to carry out the filth from the Holy Place frames impurity as a tangible corrupting presence that has accumulated in the sacred space, requiring deliberate inner and outward consecration to remove.
set.keywords: ["consecration active", "purity restoring", "defilement accumulated", "will mobilized", "sacred violated", "cleansing required"]

set.analysis_note: Persistent unfaithfulness expressed through following national abominations led the leadership to defile the holy house, showing defilement as the corporate fruit of habitual defection.
set.keywords: ["defection habitual", "heart unfaithful", "will following", "defilement corporate", "sacred desecrated", "abomination embraced"]

set.analysis_note: The land is described as saturated with the impurity of its peoples, meaning the surrounding culture has impregnated the moral environment with contagious defilement that threatens those entering it.
set.keywords: ["impurity contagious", "defilement environmental", "moral contamination", "will warned", "conscience alerted", "pollution pervasive"]

set.analysis_note: The rhetorical question asserts the utter impossibility of a clean thing arising from what is unclean, pointing to the constitutional condition of the human inner being as inherently compromised.
set.keywords: ["unclean constitutional", "nature defiled", "purity impossible", "origin corrupt", "human condition", "cleansing absent"]

set.analysis_note: Enemy nations have invaded and defiled the holy temple, depicting defilement as an act of violent desecration imposed from outside that destroys the sacred order.
The people became unclean through their own idolatrous acts, with defilement presented as the self-generated consequence of actions that constitute spiritual whoredom.
set.keywords: ["defilement self-incurred", "will acting", "idolatry chosen", "heart unfaithful", "defection spiritual", "corruption acquired"]

set.analysis_note: The unclean and the clean share the same mortal fate, suggesting that external unclean status does not alter one's existential condition before death, leveling all distinctions.
set.keywords: ["unclean status", "fate shared", "distinction dissolved", "condition moral", "mortality equalizing", "purity ineffective"]

set.analysis_note: Confronted by divine holiness, the prophet's conscience names his own lips — the organ of speech and confession — as unclean, revealing inner defilement through self-aware moral collapse.
set.keywords: ["conscience naming", "self-awareness moral", "unclean lips", "holiness confronting", "guilt surfacing", "inner being exposed"]

set.analysis_note: The will is urged to actively reject and declare the idols defiled, with defilement here becoming a deliberate moral verdict the person passes upon what was previously valued and worshipped.
set.keywords: ["will rejecting", "idols defiled", "repudiation active", "moral verdict", "heart turning", "devotion reversed"]

set.analysis_note: The unclean are excluded from the holy Way, positioning moral impurity as a state that bars the inner person from the path of holiness and divine restoration.
set.keywords: ["unclean excluded", "holiness bounded", "access denied", "moral state barring", "purity required", "restoration conditional"]

set.analysis_note: The promise that the uncircumcised and unclean will no longer enter the holy city points to a future where inner and ritual defilement is permanently excluded from God's restored presence.
set.keywords: ["unclean excluded", "future cleansing", "defilement permanent", "presence guarded", "holiness restored", "exclusion eschatological"]

set.analysis_note: The command to touch no unclean thing and to purify oneself before bearing the sacred vessels demands that the inner person be separated from defilement as an act of deliberate will and consecration.
set.keywords: ["will separating", "defilement avoided", "purity demanded", "consecration active", "conscience engaged", "sacred guarded"]

set.analysis_note: The corporate confession that all have become like the unclean and that even righteous deeds are polluted garments reveals a total inner defilement that invalidates moral performance before God.
Having received the land as gift, the people's entry brought defilement upon it, showing that inner corruption expressed through abominable practices contaminates even what God consecrated.
set.keywords: ["defilement spreading", "will unfaithful", "gift desecrated", "ingratitude active", "heart corrupting", "land polluted"]

set.analysis_note: The denial 'I am not unclean' is challenged by the visible evidence of idolatrous pursuit, exposing a self-deceived will that refuses to acknowledge its own defiled condition.
Placing detestable idols in the named house of God is framed as a deliberate act of defilement, showing the will purposefully desecrating the sacred space through idolatrous installation.
set.keywords: ["defilement deliberate", "will defiant", "sacred desecrated", "idols enthroned", "heart rebellious", "sanctuary violated"]

set.analysis_note: Houses where idolatrous offerings were made are declared defiled like Topheth, showing that the idolatrous acts performed in them transfer the unclean status of that most profane place onto the dwelling.
set.keywords: ["defilement transferred", "idolatry practiced", "sacred profaned", "moral status acquired", "dwelling corrupted", "judgment declared"]

set.analysis_note: Placing abominations in God's house to defile it repeats the pattern of willful desecration of the sacred, underscoring the deliberate and persistent nature of the inner rebellion.
set.keywords: ["defilement deliberate", "will defiant", "sacred desecrated", "rebellion persistent", "heart hardening", "idols installed"]

set.analysis_note: Jerusalem's designation as a filthy thing among the nations signals that judgment has made the city an object of impurity-revulsion, exposing the corporate inner defilement that brought on divine abandonment.
set.keywords: ["defilement corporate", "judgment expressed", "abandonment divine", "shame public", "impurity status", "isolation imposed"]

set.analysis_note: The cry 'Unclean! Away!' directed at the exiles treats them as contagiously defiled, showing how inner-moral and social defilement leads to total expulsion and relational severance.
set.keywords: ["unclean declared", "expulsion social", "defilement contagious", "isolation forced", "shame bearing", "rejection total"]

set.analysis_note: Eating unclean bread in exile among the nations is the divinely ordained consequence of prior defilement, projecting the inner-moral pollution outward into the physical and social condition of the exiles.
set.keywords: ["defilement consequential", "judgment embodied", "unclean eating", "pollution extended", "exile condition", "divine sentence"]

set.analysis_note: The prophet's protest — that he has never defiled himself with prohibited food — reveals conscience actively defending the inner person's maintained purity as a matter of lifelong personal integrity.
set.keywords: ["conscience defending", "purity maintained", "will preserving", "defilement resisted", "integrity lifelong", "protest moral"]

set.analysis_note: Defiling God's sanctuary with detestable abominations provokes divine withdrawal of compassion, making the defilement of the sacred an act that directly severs the covenantal bond.
set.keywords: ["sanctuary defiled", "covenant severed", "divine withdrawal", "defilement provocative", "will abominable", "judgment merited"]

set.analysis_note: Gold and silver become like an unclean thing on the day of wrath because they were the stumbling block of iniquity — wealth that served as the vehicle of spiritual impurity is exposed as worthless and defiling.
set.keywords: ["wealth defiling", "idolatry stumbling", "impurity material", "desire corrupting", "iniquity rooted", "judgment revealing"]

set.analysis_note: Beautiful ornament turned to prideful idolatry is declared by God to be an unclean thing, showing that pride-driven misuse of the good transforms it into a source of defilement.
set.keywords: ["pride corrupting", "idolatry made", "beauty defiled", "heart perverted", "divine verdict", "impurity assigned"]

set.analysis_note: The divine command to defile the house by filling its courts with the slain turns the sacred space into a site of judgment-death, using defilement as the instrument of divine wrath.
set.keywords: ["defilement commanded", "judgment enacted", "sacred overturned", "wrath active", "death filling", "holiness withdrawn"]

set.analysis_note: The goal of divine discipline is that the house may cease to go astray and defile themselves with transgressions, framing self-defilement as the inner expression of a will repeatedly straying from God.
set.keywords: ["self-defilement habitual", "will straying", "covenant restored", "defection repeated", "heart returning", "defilement ending"]

set.analysis_note: Not defiling a neighbor's wife is listed among the marks of the righteous man, positioning sexual fidelity as an expression of the will's refusal to defile another person and oneself.
set.keywords: ["will refusing", "sexual defilement avoided", "fidelity moral", "righteousness marked", "boundary respected", "conscience active"]

set.analysis_note: Avoiding approach to a woman in her menstrual impurity is named as part of the righteous man's conduct, with impurity marking a physical-ritual boundary that the disciplined will honors.
set.keywords: ["impurity boundary", "will honoring", "ritual restraint", "conscience observing", "purity respected", "righteousness evidenced"]

set.analysis_note: The wicked son defiles his neighbor's wife, presenting sexual defilement as a mark of the will's active violation of others, contrasting with the righteous father's restraint.
set.keywords: ["will violating", "defilement sexual", "violation active", "contrast moral", "wickedness chosen", "neighbor wronged"]

set.analysis_note: Not defiling a neighbor's wife marks the third-generation righteous man, confirming that personal moral integrity requires the will's consistent refusal of sexual defilement regardless of family heritage.
set.keywords: ["will refusing", "defilement avoided", "integrity personal", "righteousness sustained", "sexual boundary", "conscience consistent"]

set.analysis_note: The command not to defile themselves with the idols of Egypt calls for an inner rejection of what the eyes feast upon, demanding that desire be redirected away from idolatrous attachment.
set.keywords: ["desire redirected", "will commanded", "idolatry rejected", "defilement voluntary", "eyes guarded", "heart loyalty"]

set.analysis_note: The wilderness generation's children are warned not to defile themselves with their fathers' idols, framing defilement by idolatry as an inherited temptation requiring active refusal of the will.
set.keywords: ["will warned", "idolatry inherited", "defilement refused", "conscience alerted", "generational pattern", "defection avoided"]

set.analysis_note: God himself defiles them through their own gifts by allowing the sacrifice of firstborn, using their defiant self-defilement as a judicial instrument of devastation and revelation of his lordship.
set.keywords: ["defilement judicial", "divine judgment", "gifts perverted", "heart hardening", "sovereignty revealed", "devastation through sin"]

set.analysis_note: The rhetorical challenge — will you defile yourselves after your fathers' manner? — exposes the temptation of generational repetition and calls the will to break from the inherited pattern of defilement.
set.keywords: ["will challenged", "defilement generational", "pattern repeated", "defection habitual", "conscience provoked", "choice confronted"]

set.analysis_note: Ongoing self-defilement through idolatry — including child sacrifice — results in God refusing to be inquired of, showing that persistent defilement closes the relational access between the defiled will and God.
set.keywords: ["defilement ongoing", "will persisting", "access closed", "divine rejection", "idolatry active", "child sacrifice"]

set.analysis_note: Future remembrance of the deeds by which they defiled themselves will produce self-loathing, revealing that the memory of defilement serves as the inner mechanism of repentance and moral grief.
The city makes idols purposefully to defile herself, presenting defilement as a deliberate and self-directed act of the corporate will that hastens its own time of judgment.
Guilt through bloodshed and defilement through idolatry are paired as the twin results of the city's choices, with defilement marking the moral-spiritual status acquired through those acts.
set.keywords: ["defilement acquired", "guilt paired", "blood shed", "idols made", "moral status", "judgment brought near"]

set.analysis_note: The city's name is declared defiled among the nations, showing that the inner corruption of a community becomes a publicly visible moral identity that invites shame and mockery.
set.keywords: ["defilement public", "shame named", "reputation corrupted", "moral identity", "corporate unclean", "judgment visible"]

set.analysis_note: Men violating women who are in menstrual impurity compounds sexual defilement by transgressing the boundary the impure state itself defines, intensifying the moral rupture.
set.keywords: ["violation sexual", "impurity boundary", "defilement compounded", "will transgressing", "boundary broken", "moral rupture"]

set.analysis_note: The menstrual impurity of the women marks a boundary that the violating men cross, making their act a transgression against a boundary defined by the body's own impure state.
set.keywords: ["impurity boundary", "body state", "boundary transgressed", "violation active", "will transgressing", "defilement sexual"]

set.analysis_note: Multiple acts of sexual defilement — with neighbor's wife, daughter-in-law, and sister — are catalogued together, showing the will's wide-ranging defilement of the nearest relational bonds.
set.keywords: ["will violating", "defilement sexual", "relational bonds broken", "abomination multiple", "conscience absent", "lewdness active"]

set.analysis_note: The priests' failure to teach the distinction between unclean and clean is presented as a collapse of moral-spiritual discernment in those responsible for guarding the community's inner formation.
set.keywords: ["discernment failed", "distinction lost", "conscience unformed", "priestly failure", "moral teaching absent", "profaning"]

set.analysis_note: She defiles herself with every idol of those after whom she lusts, showing defilement as the inward consequence of desire-driven idolatrous attachment that the will pursues without restraint.
set.keywords: ["desire unrestrained", "will pursuing", "defilement self-incurred", "idolatry lusted", "attachment corrupting", "heart defiled"]

set.analysis_note: God sees that she was defiled and that both sisters walked the same way, revealing defilement as a spiritually observable condition that God discerns and holds as evidence of shared guilt.
set.keywords: ["defilement observed", "divine perception", "guilt shared", "heart exposed", "defection mirrored", "condition confirmed"]

set.analysis_note: External agents defile her through their lust, and having been defiled she turns from them in disgust, showing that defilement can be received through corrupting relations and then generate its own revulsion.
set.keywords: ["defilement received", "lust corrupting", "revulsion following", "will turning", "disgust moral", "defilement relational"]

set.analysis_note: Playing the whore with the nations and defiling oneself with their idols is named as the direct cause of judgment, linking the will's chosen alliances to the defilement that brought destruction.
Defiling the sanctuary and profaning the Sabbaths on the same day presents the compound defilement of both sacred space and sacred time as evidence of the full extent of the will's apostasy.
set.keywords: ["sanctuary defiled", "sabbath profaned", "defilement compound", "will apostate", "sacred violated", "rebellion full"]

set.analysis_note: Defiling a neighbor's wife is listed alongside reliance on the sword and abominations as reasons the land cannot be possessed, pairing sexual defilement with the broader pattern of violent moral corruption.
set.keywords: ["defilement sexual", "abomination paired", "moral corruption", "will violating", "possession forfeited", "wickedness multiple"]

set.analysis_note: The people's habitual conduct defiled the land they lived in, and God perceived their ways as morally repugnant as menstrual impurity — an inner disposition expressed outwardly in persistent corrupting behaviour.
The people's ways before God were compared to menstrual impurity, signalling a state of moral uncleanness that made their conduct utterly repellent to holiness.
Idolatrous practice defiled the land and provoked divine wrath, showing that inner allegiance given to false worship produced a corrupting contamination of sacred space.
Future restoration involves the people no longer defiling themselves with idols and transgressions — a promise of inner cleansing that removes habitual corrupting loyalty to false things.
set.keywords: ["defilement ceased", "idolatry abandoned", "cleansing promised", "loyalty redirected", "will turning", "habit broken"]

set.analysis_note: In the restored order, the people will no longer defile God's holy name through whoring and association with death — the holy name is vulnerable to defilement by wilful, persistent unfaithfulness.
set.keywords: ["name defiled", "unfaithfulness wilful", "holiness proximity", "whoring habitual", "will restrained future"]

set.analysis_note: The people defiled God's holy name by placing their own thresholds adjacent to his, treating the sacred as common through their abominations, a presumptuous violation that consumed them in divine anger.
Priests are charged to teach the people to discern the unclean from the clean, indicating that moral and ritual discernment is a faculty that must be cultivated through instruction.

Priests must guard their purity by avoiding contact with the dead, though close family bereavement is an exception — defilement here is a bodily-ritual state with relational and vocational consequences.
God's comprehensive knowledge of the people reveals their defilement as the outcome of spiritual whoredom — the inner state of unfaithfulness is fully visible and produces a corrupt condition.
Widespread whoredom within the covenant community has left the people in a state of defilement, described as a horrible thing perceived by God — inner corruption made visible and morally revolting.

Exile from the holy land means eating unclean food in foreign nations — the inner-being here is minimal; the verse primarily describes ritual/categorical uncleanness as a consequence of being removed from sacred space.

Eating bread outside the holy community defiles those who partake, because their worship is cut off — defilement here flows from disconnection from covenant relationship, making even sustenance spiritually contaminating.

Dying in an unclean land is the judgment pronounced on the one who silenced the prophet — exile to foreign, ritually impure territory is a consequence that marks the condemned as cut off from the holy.
The uncleanness attached to the land because of the people's sin makes it no place for rest — moral defilement has a corrosive, destructive force that disqualifies the space for habitation.
Contact with a corpse-defiled person transmits impurity to whatever they touch — defilement is portrayed as a contagious condition that passes outward from an already unclean person.
A person rendered unclean by contact with death carries that state outward to everything touched — the unclean condition is active, communicable, and categorical.
The people's inner moral state renders all their offerings unclean before God — the impurity originates within the community and corrupts every act of worship they perform.
set.keywords: ["inner state corrupting", "offering defiled", "worship unacceptable", "community impure", "moral origin"]

set.analysis_note: A fountain opened for cleansing will remove sin and impurity, promising that the deep moral uncleanness of the community can be washed away through a divinely provided source of purification.
Unclean spirits represent hostile, morally corrupting powers over which authority is granted — the term names a category of spiritual agents that defile and afflict persons.
An unclean spirit displaced from a person wanders restlessly, seeking re-entry — the spirit's nature is characterized by impurity and by an active drive to inhabit and corrupt a human person.
The inner life of the hypocrites is full of impurity despite an outwardly attractive appearance — their defilement is located within, hidden from human sight but utterly contrary to genuine holiness.
A man in the synagogue possessed by an unclean spirit is presented as an inhabited, controlled person whose inner state is defined by the corrupting spiritual presence within him.
The unclean spirit manifests its departure through violent convulsion and outcry, showing that its presence had imposed a controlling, afflicting force on the person's inner and bodily life.

The commanding authority over unclean spirits evokes amazement — the spirits' obedience reveals they are subject to a higher power, highlighting their nature as defiling agents under authoritative restraint.
Unclean spirits recognize and confess a superior divine identity before they are commanded — their acknowledgment reveals self-awareness alongside their defiling, hostile nature.
Opponents attribute Jesus's power to an unclean spirit, a charge that misidentifies divine action as moral corruption — the accusation attempts to locate defilement at the source of his authority.
A man living among tombs had an unclean spirit — his dwelling in a place of death and his possession together mark him as utterly cut off from clean, communal life.
The direct command to the unclean spirit to leave the man defines the spirit as a distinct, addressable entity whose presence constitutes the man's defilement.
The unclean spirits leave the man and enter a herd of pigs which rush to destruction — their transfer and the resulting death illustrate the destructive, corrupting force of their impurity.
Delegated authority over unclean spirits is given to the twelve — the verse frames unclean spirits as entities that must be actively opposed and overcome, requiring granted power.
A child afflicted by an unclean spirit drives her mother to seek help — the spirit's presence is experienced as a tormenting, controlling affliction requiring external intervention.
The unclean spirit is rebuked and commanded never to re-enter — its capacity to suppress speech and hearing shows it impairs the inner and bodily faculties of the person it inhabits.
A man in the synagogue had the spirit of an unclean demon — the compound phrase intensifies the defiling, hostile quality of the spiritual presence controlling him.

Amazement follows the commanding power over unclean spirits — the spirits' compliance underscores that unclean spiritual forces yield to authoritative speech, revealing defilement as ultimately subject to divine power.
Those troubled by unclean spirits receive healing — the verse equates being freed from an unclean spirit with a restoration of wholeness, treating spiritual defilement as a form of affliction.
The unclean spirit had repeatedly seized the man, overpowering all human restraint — its persistent, irresistible control illustrates total domination of the person's will and bodily agency.
The unclean spirit convulses a child until rebuked and expelled — its violent, destructive action against the body evidences its corrupting hold on the person.
An unclean spirit driven out seeks rest and plans return — its restless agency and will to re-inhabit a person show it as an active, purposeful defiling force.
People afflicted with unclean spirits are brought for healing — the verse presents unclean spiritual possession as a condition of suffering that healing reverses.
Unclean spirits cry out as they are expelled from many — their vocal resistance at departure indicates a defiling force that resists removal and has gripped multiple persons.
Peter's refusal to eat anything common or unclean reflects a deeply formed conscience-level conviction about ritual and moral purity that resists the divine vision urging change.
God's revelation overturns the learned judgment that persons of other nations are unclean — inner categories of who is defiled are shown to be humanly constructed and subject to divine correction.
Peter recounts his firm refusal to eat anything common or unclean — the repeated conviction shows a conscience deeply shaped by purity categories that he had never overridden.
God gives people over to the impurity that arises from heart-level lusts — impurity here is the condition to which unchecked desire leads, seated in the heart and expressed in bodily dishonour.

Members once enslaved to impurity and lawlessness are now called to offer themselves to righteousness — impurity is framed as a prior master that directed bodily members toward escalating moral disorder.

Children of a believer are considered holy rather than unclean because of the sanctifying influence of the believing parent — unclean here refers to a relational, covenantal status rather than personal moral guilt.
A weak conscience is defiled when someone eats food associated with idols without genuine moral conviction — defilement originates in acting against conscience, not in the food itself.
set.keywords: ["conscience defiled", "weakness moral", "conviction absent", "defilement inner", "action violating conscience"]

set.analysis_note: The call to separation from unclean things is the condition for divine welcome — touching what is unclean is a relational and moral barrier that must be refused for restored communion with God.
Defilement is located in both body and spirit and must be actively cleansed — the call to self-purification treats defilement as penetrating the full inner-outer constitution of the person.
set.keywords: ["defilement bodily", "defilement spiritual", "self cleansing", "holiness pursued", "will active", "spirit purified"]

set.analysis_note: Impurity is named alongside sexual immorality and sensuality as sins from which those who practised them have not repented — the impurity is morally chosen, persistent, and unrepented, revealing a hardened will.

Impurity is listed as a visible work of the flesh — an inner impulse of the lower nature that manifests outwardly, sitting alongside sexual immorality and sensuality as a product of unrestrained bodily desire.
Callousness of conscience has led to a willing self-surrender to sensuality, with impurity practiced greedily — showing the will actively pursuing corruption once conscience has been silenced.

Impurity, alongside sexual immorality and covetousness, must not even be spoken of among those set apart — it is incompatible with a holy inner identity and must be excluded from both conduct and speech.
set.keywords: ["identity holy", "purity demanded", "desire excluded", "speech reflecting", "conduct incompatible", "will aligned"]

set.analysis_note: Being impure is equated with idolatry and disqualifies a person from divine inheritance — the unclean inner state is a heart allegiance disorder that displaces God with corrupt desire.
set.keywords: ["heart allegiance", "desire displacing", "idolatry interior", "inheritance forfeited", "soul orientation", "will corrupted"]

set.analysis_note: Impurity is listed among earthly inner drives that must be mortified — it belongs to the old self's passion and desire complex, rooted in the body's earthly orientation and linked to covetousness as idolatry.
set.keywords: ["earthly orientation", "desire mortified", "passion complex", "will mortifying", "body sourced", "self old"]

set.analysis_note: The appeal described here is explicitly not driven by impurity — meaning right motivation before God requires an inner life free from morally corrupt desires contaminating one's motives.
set.keywords: ["motive pure", "desire absent", "integrity inner", "conscience clean", "will uncorrupted", "motivation tested"]

set.analysis_note: Impurity is the state God's calling opposes — the divine call is directed toward holiness, placing impurity as the contrary inner condition that defines what the redeemed life must leave behind.
set.keywords: ["calling opposed", "holiness contrasted", "condition left", "inner state opposed", "vocation holy", "purity demanded"]

set.analysis_note: Defilement here is tied to indulgence in defiling passion combined with contempt for authority — the inner posture is one of bold wilfulness, where desire and pride suppress reverence and restraint.
set.keywords: ["passion indulged", "authority despised", "will bold", "reverence suppressed", "defiance wilful", "desire corrupting", "restraint absent"]

set.analysis_note: A few have not soiled their garments — meaning they have kept their inner and outward life undefiled amid a compromised community, and this moral integrity is the basis of their worthiness to walk with Christ.
set.keywords: ["integrity preserved", "purity maintained", "will holding", "worthiness earned", "conscience unsoiled", "defection resisted"]

set.analysis_note: These have not defiled themselves — their bodies and inner lives have been kept pure, expressing total consecration to the Lamb, with self-denial and devotion as the inner posture that defines their identity.
set.keywords: ["consecration total", "purity bodily", "self denial", "devotion complete", "will yielded", "defilement absent"]

set.analysis_note: Three unclean spirits emerge from corrupt spiritual sources — the term here marks these as beings whose inner character is morally and spiritually defiled, functioning as agents of deception sourced in darkness.
The cup held contains impurities of sexual immorality alongside abominations — the inner corruption is displayed externally through seductive beauty, masking a soul saturated with moral filth.
set.keywords: ["soul saturated", "corruption masked", "desire abominable", "seduction concealing", "filth interior", "immorality displayed"]

set.analysis_note: The fallen city has become a habitation of every unclean spirit and creature — spiritual and moral uncleanliness is now the defining inner and environmental character of total corruption and ruin.
set.keywords: ["corruption total", "spirit unclean", "habitation defiled", "ruin moral", "character defined", "darkness dwelling"]
